text:
On knowing and acting:

I watched @NJHagens 's video on information burnout today and I've been thinking about how it is important to read up on "the issues" just enough that you are not ignorant or tone-deaf, but also not triggered. 

I also thought about how networks of trust are important because when you learn from friends and colleagues, they will have your best interests at heart instead of learning from someone you'll probably never meet and have agendas of their own.

Lastly, I thank Nate for being one of the few who talks about the issues and then offers a showcase of people who are responding to the predicament we face.

Part of my theory of change is that when you give practical examples of agency to people, they will follow suit. I don't think it's enough to make people feel good, they have to witness good as well.

The showcase I'm referring to:
https://t.co/RzkZyhN1I5

I’ve come to terms that any attempt to revitalize the arts and humanities will get me labelled as a reactionary

But I also thinking fighting colonialist studies is futile. I don’t have much of a problem with colonialist studies other than it could be overfitted and over applied. Instead it needs to be reintegrated instead of denigrated. The alternative comes with some listening and understanding while leaving room for experimentation

We can have room for classics AND Baldwin (i.e social commentary literature)

The teachers that I want to see in my network and project immediately get this

Thank you to everyone who attended the first Toronto Fractal gathering last night!!!
Deeply humbled by everyone showing up and showing out! A beautiful blend of love, play, and agency.

And of course, there will be more to come in case you missed it!

-Nailed the speech I was struggling with the days leading up
-Ducky shirt was a hit
-Got to play Never Have I Ever in a big group
-Great Q’s on bristol boards!

text:
As an organizer, I plan on interacting with awkward people more gracefully. Here’s what I’ve been doing:
-If I see someone by themselves, introduce myself and have a short, good chat with them. They really just need one good interaction, one W to get going
-Point them to people who have similar interests and projects to them
-If possible, make an intro for them if they have difficulty communicating their intentions

Ideally, participants do this on their own accord, but yeah may the good vibes roll!

text:
Celebrating and Sunsetting

Last night (Jun 3rd), we celebrated the one year anniversary of Weave Toronto

We ran two semesters with 11 classes in total (4 in the first and 7 in the second) and countless weeklies of different types from bar hangs to lightning talks to dinners and more

One of my main intents was to build a collective that connects the intellectual communities of the city together so they don’t operate in silos and that the courses would reflect the ideological diversity the city has to offer

In some ways it was a success in terms of the way how participants found themselves interwoven in multiple communities, but there was also a part of me that wished for a tighter core group but people found themselves in and out due to life circumstances whether it be travel, new work/projects, priority shifts, etc

Toronto has this unique ephermerality but also stability that you only know when you experience it. You can bump into the same person three times a week then none at all for a few weeks and rinse and repeat.

I came in with a rollercoaster of emotions because **it would be the last event under the Weave banner**. Yes you heard it here, we are sunsetting the experiment. It may be taken upon by someone else, but we (the original founders that consist of myself, Phil, and Yuli) are going to go our separate ways.

There’s many thanks to go around and I’ll add them in the replies, but here are some things I’m grateful for in the past year:

-Countless friends made and some deepening of relationships along the way
-Working on my hosting and public speaking skills
-Meeting so many interesting people and events
-Making courses and events with a bunch of people I admire and deeply respect
-Being a part of a beautiful evolving scene

A lot of you may feel sad or angered or confused by this news but I’ll reassure you with the fact that I will still be around supporting the Toronto events scene

I will be at various communities like @_torontosociety and @adamchenchew ‘s walks and helping people belong. My bet is that if you are abundant in belonging, you will help others belong. I also see myself tinkering with event curation infrastructure, but for the most part I will be settling down and enjoying the small things in life like cooking and music jamming at parks

Thank you to all who have been on this journey and ride with me! 

I would love to reflect with @HiNaryan , @Prigoose and @TylerAlterman….let’s make it happen!

To close…Weave the org may be gone, but Weave the process and network is NEVER LEAVING!!!!
